module.title: MATH & ALGORITHMS
module.subtitle: math lib · recursion · table.sort
module.theory:
## The math toolbox
Lua ships a built-in **math** library. You call its tools with a dot: `math.floor(x)`. The handy ones:

- **math.floor(x)** — round DOWN to the nearest whole number. `math.floor(3.9)` is `3`.
- **math.abs(x)** — distance from zero (drops the minus sign). `math.abs(-7)` is `7`.
- **math.max(a, b, ...)** — the biggest of the arguments.
- **math.min(a, b, ...)** — the smallest.
- **math.sqrt(x)** — the square root. `math.sqrt(9)` is `3.0`.

~~~lua
print(math.floor(3.9))   -- 3
print(math.abs(-7))      -- 7
print(math.max(4, 9, 2)) -- 9
print(math.sqrt(16))     -- 4.0
~~~

> INTEL — `math.sqrt` always hands back a float, so `math.sqrt(9)` prints as `3.0`, not `3`. That is normal — `3.0 == 3` is still `true` in Lua.

## Recursion: a function that calls itself
**Recursion** means a function calls itself on a smaller problem until it hits a tiny case it can answer directly — the **base case**. Without a base case it loops forever, so always write that first.

The classic is **factorial** (`5! = 5*4*3*2*1`):
~~~lua
function factorial(n)
  if n <= 1 then return 1 end      -- base case
  return n * factorial(n - 1)      -- shrink the problem
end
print(factorial(5))  -- 120
~~~

**Euclid's algorithm** finds the greatest common divisor (gcd) — the largest number that divides both. Lua's `%` gives the remainder:
~~~lua
function gcd(a, b)
  if b == 0 then return a end      -- base case
  return gcd(b, a % b)             -- swap & shrink
end
print(gcd(48, 18))  -- 6
~~~

**Fibonacci** adds the two numbers before it (0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, ...). Two base cases here:
~~~lua
function fib(n)
  if n < 2 then return n end       -- fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1
  return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2)
end
print(fib(7))  -- 13
~~~

> WARNING — Every recursion needs a base case that stops it. If `factorial` never checked `n <= 1` it would call itself forever and crash with a stack overflow.

## Checking for primes
A **prime** is a whole number greater than 1 divisible only by 1 and itself (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, ...). To test `n`, try dividing by every number from 2 up: if any divides evenly (remainder 0), it is NOT prime.
~~~lua
function is_prime(n)
  if n < 2 then return false end
  for d = 2, n - 1 do
    if n % d == 0 then return false end
  end
  return true
end
~~~

## Sorting a table
**table.sort(t)** rearranges an array IN PLACE — it changes `t` itself and returns nothing. By default it sorts ascending (smallest first):
~~~lua
local nums = {5, 1, 4, 2}
table.sort(nums)
print(nums[1], nums[4])  -- 1   5
~~~

To sort differently, pass a **comparator**: a function `(a, b)` that returns `true` when `a` should come before `b`. For descending order, say "a comes first when it is bigger":
~~~lua
local nums = {5, 1, 4, 2}
table.sort(nums, function(a, b) return a > b end)
print(nums[1])  -- 5
~~~

> WARNING — `table.sort` does NOT return a new table. Writing `local s = table.sort(t)` puts `nil` in `s`. Sort `t`, then use `t`. Also remember Lua arrays start at index **1**, not 0 — the first element is `t[1]`.

exercises.title: DRIFT GAP
exercises.prompt:
Define **drift_gap(target, actual)** that returns how far `actual` is from `target`, as a positive number, using **math.abs**.
~~~lua
drift_gap(10, 7)  -- 3
drift_gap(7, 10)  -- 3
~~~
exercises.starter:
function drift_gap(target, actual)
  -- TODO: use math.abs
  return 0
end
exercises.hint: math.abs(target - actual) drops the minus sign.
exercises.lore: Off by three microns. The net notices.

exercises.title: PEAK SIGNAL
exercises.prompt:
Define **peak_signal(a, b, c)** that returns the largest of the three numbers, using **math.max**.
~~~lua
peak_signal(4, 9, 2)  -- 9
~~~
exercises.starter:
function peak_signal(a, b, c)
  -- TODO: use math.max
  return 0
end
exercises.hint: math.max takes any number of arguments and returns the biggest.

exercises.title: FLOOR VOLTS
exercises.prompt:
Define **floor_volts(reading)** that returns the reading rounded DOWN to a whole number, using **math.floor**.
~~~lua
floor_volts(3.9)   -- 3
floor_volts(7.01)  -- 7
~~~
exercises.starter:
function floor_volts(reading)
  -- TODO: use math.floor
  return reading
end
exercises.hint: math.floor(3.9) is 3 — it always rounds toward smaller.
exercises.lore: Trim the noise. Keep the whole units.

exercises.title: VECTOR LENGTH
exercises.prompt:
Define **vector_len(x, y)** that returns the straight-line length of the vector — the square root of `x*x + y*y` — using **math.sqrt**.
~~~lua
vector_len(3, 4)  -- 5.0
~~~
exercises.starter:
function vector_len(x, y)
  -- TODO: use math.sqrt
  return 0
end
exercises.hint: math.sqrt(x*x + y*y). Squaring before adding, like Pythagoras.
exercises.lore: Distance is just geometry under pressure.

exercises.title: FACTORIAL
exercises.prompt:
Define **factorial(n)** using **recursion**. It returns `n * (n-1) * ... * 1`. The factorial of 0 is 1.
~~~lua
factorial(5)  -- 120
factorial(0)  -- 1
~~~
exercises.starter:
function factorial(n)
  -- TODO: base case, then recurse
  return 0
end
exercises.hint: Base case: if n <= 1 return 1. Otherwise return n * factorial(n - 1).
exercises.lore: Each layer multiplies the one below.

exercises.title: EUCLID GCD
exercises.prompt:
Define **gcd(a, b)** using **Euclid's algorithm** (recursion): when `b` is 0, return `a`; otherwise return `gcd(b, a % b)`.
~~~lua
gcd(48, 18)  -- 6
gcd(7, 0)    -- 7
~~~
exercises.starter:
function gcd(a, b)
  -- TODO: base case b == 0, then recurse
  return 0
end
exercises.hint: If b == 0 return a; else return gcd(b, a % b). The % is the remainder.
exercises.lore: Strip the shared factor. What remains is irreducible.

exercises.title: FIB WAVE
exercises.brief: Each term sums the two before.
exercises.prompt:
Define **fib(n)** using **recursion**. The sequence is 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, ... so `fib(0)` is 0 and `fib(1)` is 1; otherwise `fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)`.
~~~lua
fib(7)  -- 13
fib(0)  -- 0
~~~
exercises.starter:
function fib(n)
  -- TODO: base cases for 0 and 1, then recurse
  return 0
end
exercises.hint: If n < 2 return n. Otherwise return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2).
exercises.lore: The spiral grows from what came before.

exercises.title: RANK DESCENDING
exercises.prompt:
Define **rank_desc(t)** that sorts the array `t` from largest to smallest and returns it. Use **table.sort** with a comparator. Remember `table.sort` changes `t` in place, so sort it then `return t`.
~~~lua
rank_desc({3, 1, 4, 1, 5})  -- {5, 4, 3, 1, 1}
~~~
exercises.starter:
function rank_desc(t)
  -- TODO: sort t descending with a comparator, then return it
  return t
end
exercises.solution:
function rank_desc(t)
  table.sort(t, function(a, b) return a > b end)
  return t
end

tests.name: five elements, descending
tests.code:
local r = rank_desc({3, 1, 4, 1, 5})
assert(#r == 5 and r[1] == 5 and r[2] == 4 and r[3] == 3 and r[4] == 1 and r[5] == 1, "expected {5,4,3,1,1}")

tests.name: already descending stays put
tests.code:
local r = rank_desc({9, 6, 2})
assert(#r == 3 and r[1] == 9 and r[2] == 6 and r[3] == 2, "expected {9,6,2}")

tests.name: handles negatives
tests.code:
local r = rank_desc({-1, -5, 0})
assert(#r == 3 and r[1] == 0 and r[2] == -1 and r[3] == -5, "expected {0,-1,-5}")

exercises.hint: table.sort(t, function(a, b) return a > b end), then return t.
exercises.lore: The strongest signals rise to the top of the stack.
